title: Aion Clients — Caller-Side SDKs (Rust, Python, TypeScript, Gleam)
intention: Ship the caller-side SDKs for the workflow-caller role: aion-client (Rust, crates.io), aion-client-python (PyPI), aion-client-typescript (npm), and aion_client (Gleam, Hex). Each connects to an aion-server deployment and exposes the same seven operations — connect, start, signal, query, cancel, list, describe, subscribe — in the idioms of its language. A developer who has never read the engine internals should add a dependency, point at a server URL, and start a workflow with full type-checking in their language within minutes; a frontend developer using the TypeScript client should never see beamr, Gleam, or Rust. The four SDKs must behave identically where behaviour is observable, proven by a shared language-neutral behavioural contract and a conformance suite that exercises every SDK against a real server.
problem: Application teams live in Python, TypeScript, Gleam, and Rust; hand-rolling HTTP against the engine four times over guarantees four subtly different and subtly wrong clients.

Workflow operations are not plain CRUD: start may be idempotent on a caller key, a signal must reach a specific run, a query is a synchronous round-trip that may time out, cancellation is a request not an immediate kill, and event subscription is a long-lived stream that must survive transient disconnects without losing or duplicating events. Each has one correct behaviour and many plausible-but-wrong ones.

There is a hard boundary: the clients do not define the network protocol. The aion-server API (HTTP/gRPC + WebSocket) and the aion-proto wire types are owned by cluster AW; the clients consume them and must not invent fields, endpoints, or semantics the server does not provide, or they will work only against a mock.

These are public packages on four registries with four toolchains; production-ready means idiomatic, typed, documented, and published per ecosystem — not one Rust crate transliterated into three other languages.

solution.summary: A shared language-neutral behavioural contract plus four idiomatic implementations of it, all consuming the aion-proto wire types and the aion-server API. The contract (CLIENT-CONTRACT.md) pins down the meaning, inputs, outputs, errors, and hard-case semantics (start idempotency, run targeting, query timeout, cancellation-as-request, stream resumption) of the seven operations; a shared conformance scenario set is its executable form. Each SDK exposes connect, start (returning a WorkflowHandle bundling WorkflowId + RunId and per-workflow methods), signal, query, cancel, list, describe, and subscribe (a native async event stream that resumes transparently across disconnects via the per-event sequence number). Each offers a typed payload front door (serde generics in Rust, JSON/model in Python, generics in TS, encoder/decoder in Gleam) over an always-present raw-Payload escape hatch, and maps server failures to a documented branchable error taxonomy. The Rust client also binds to an embedded aion engine in-process behind a feature flag, reusing the same handle and payload machinery.

decisions.title: One behavioural contract, four implementations
decisions.choice: The meaning of each operation — inputs, outputs, errors, and the hard cases (idempotency, run targeting, query timeout, cancellation, resumption) — is defined once in a language-neutral CLIENT-CONTRACT.md and shared. Each SDK expresses it idiomatically and is measured against the conformance suite. Rejected: each SDK defining its own semantics (drift). Rejected: code-generating all four from aion-proto and stopping (generated stubs give wire types but not the behavioural layer that makes an SDK worth using).

decisions.title: start returns a WorkflowHandle, not bare IDs
decisions.choice: start returns a handle bundling WorkflowId + RunId and offering the per-workflow operations (signal/query/cancel/describe/subscribe) as methods, so a caller can operate on what they just started without restating IDs. Bare-ID constructors remain for callers holding only an ID. Rejected: returning a WorkflowId string, which pushes run-targeting and ID plumbing onto every caller.

decisions.title: Seven operations, identical across SDKs
decisions.choice: Every SDK exposes connect, start, signal, query, cancel, list, describe, subscribe, named idiomatically per language, with identical observable behaviour verified by the shared conformance suite. The set is fixed by the contract; rendering is per ecosystem.

decisions.title: Typed sugar over an always-present raw Payload path
decisions.choice: Workflow inputs, signal payloads, query args, and results are Payload (bytes + content-type) on the wire. Each SDK offers a typed convenience surface (serde generics in Rust; JSON/Pydantic-or-target-type in Python; generics in TS; gleam/json encoder + gleam/dynamic decoder in Gleam), JSON-by-default, plus a raw-Payload escape hatch for non-JSON or pre-serialised payloads. Rejected: typed-only — it strands non-JSON callers.

decisions.title: Clients consume aion-proto; never define wire types
decisions.choice: Request/response shapes, the gRPC service, and the WebSocket frame format are owned by AW. The Rust client imports aion-proto; the other SDKs are generated against / hand-mapped to it. No SDK adds fields or endpoints the server does not serve. Rejected: each client carrying its own wire-type copy (drift from the server).

decisions.title: Shared, named error taxonomy, idiomatic per language
decisions.choice: The set of distinguishable failures is fixed: NotFound, AlreadyExists (idempotency conflict), QueryFailed, QueryTimeout, Cancelled, Unavailable (transport), Unauthenticated, NamespaceDenied (namespace authorization denial, with detail), InvalidArgument, Server (unexpected, with detail). Each SDK renders it idiomatically (Rust enum + thiserror; Python exception hierarchy; TS discriminated union / subclasses; Gleam custom type). No swallowed errors, no catch-all collapse. Rejected: a single opaque error per SDK — callers cannot branch on idempotency conflict vs query timeout vs auth failure.

decisions.title: subscribe resumes transparently via sequence number
decisions.choice: subscribe returns a language-native async stream of decoded events. On transient disconnect the SDK reconnects and resumes from the last delivered event's per-workflow sequence number (aion-core envelope), giving the caller a gap-free, duplicate-free stream; terminal failures surface through the stream, not as a silent end. Rejected: exposing raw socket lifecycle to the caller — recreates per-client reconnection bugs the SDKs exist to prevent.

decisions.title: start honours a caller-supplied idempotency key
decisions.choice: start accepts an optional idempotency key. A retried identical start returns the original WorkflowHandle; a conflicting reuse (same key, different request) raises AlreadyExists. This makes start safe to retry from a caller's own retry loop without double-launching a workflow.

decisions.title: Rust client doubles as the embedded binding
decisions.choice: The Rust aion-client is the network client against aion-server and, behind a feature flag, an embedded binding to an in-process aion engine. The embedded path is a thin alternate constructor targeting the engine's in-process API, reusing the same WorkflowHandle and typed-payload machinery. The network path is the default; the embedded path never pulls aion into a client that does not opt in (CO2).

decisions.title: Conformance suite is the agreement gate
decisions.choice: A single language-neutral scenario set (start -> signal -> query -> list -> describe -> cancel -> subscribe, plus error and idempotency cases) runs each SDK against a real aion-server and asserts identical observable behaviour. It is the executable form of the contract and the gate proving the four SDKs agree; it runs in each ecosystem's test toolchain against a server fixture.

constraints.description: Clients consume the aion-server API and aion-proto wire types; they SHALL NOT define their own wire formats, endpoints, or fields the server does not serve. The protocol is owned by AW.

Clients SHALL NOT depend on engine internals (aion, aion-store, beamr). The Rust client may depend on aion-core (shared domain types) and aion-proto; the embedded constructor may depend on aion only behind a feature flag, never by default.
All four SDKs expose the same seven operations with identical observable behaviour, verified by the shared conformance suite.
Every SDK exposes both a typed payload surface (per language) and a raw-Payload escape hatch; neither is removable.
Every SDK maps server failures to a documented, branchable error taxonomy. No swallowed errors, no single catch-all collapsing distinct failures.

subscribe resumes transparently across transient disconnects using the per-event sequence number, delivering a gap-free duplicate-free stream; terminal failures surface through the stream, not as a silent end.
start honours a caller-supplied idempotency key: a retried identical start returns the original handle; a conflicting reuse raises AlreadyExists.

Per-ecosystem toolchain gates pass: Rust (clippy -D warnings, fmt --check, no allow/expect/ignore), Python (ruff + mypy --strict + pytest), TypeScript (tsc strict + eslint + tests), Gleam (gleam format --check + gleam check + gleam test). No-partial-implementation and no-silent-failure standards hold across all four.
Each package is publishable to its registry (crates.io, PyPI, npm, Hex) with a README, a type-checked public surface, and a runnable example covering all seven operations.

goals: A language-neutral CLIENT-CONTRACT.md defining the seven operations' inputs, outputs, errors, and hard-case semantics (idempotency, run targeting, query timeout, cancellation-as-request, stream resumption).

aion-client (Rust): Client/ClientBuilder connect with auth + TLS; start/signal/query/cancel/list/describe; WorkflowHandle; serde-typed payloads + raw escape hatch; resuming event Stream; ClientError taxonomy; embedded binding behind a feature flag.

aion-client-python (PyPI): Client + WorkflowHandle covering all seven operations; JSON/model typed payloads + bytes escape hatch; async event iterator with resumption; exception hierarchy; mypy --strict clean.

aion-client-typescript (npm): Client + WorkflowHandle covering all seven operations; generic typed payloads + raw escape hatch; AsyncIterable event stream with resumption; error union/classes; tsc strict + eslint clean.

aion_client (Gleam, Hex): connect/start/signal/query/cancel/list/describe + handle ops; encoder/decoder typed payloads + raw escape hatch; event stream with resumption; error custom type; gleam check + format clean.
A shared conformance scenario set and per-ecosystem harnesses that run each SDK against a real aion-server and assert identical observable behaviour.
Packaging and publishing metadata, READMEs, and runnable seven-operation examples for all four registries.
non_goals: No worker SDKs — executing activities is AR (aion-worker-*). Clients drive workflows; workers execute activities.
No server or wire protocol — the HTTP/gRPC + WebSocket API and aion-proto wire types are AW. This cluster consumes them.
No engine, replay, timers, signal routing, or query dispatch (AE/AD/AT). The client sends a signal/query over the API; the engine routes and answers it.
No authoring SDK — defining workflows is aion_flow (AF). A caller starts a workflow by type name; it does not define one.
No dashboard (AU) — it may use these clients but is not designed here.
No Elixir client in this cluster — it follows once aion_flow_ex lands, mirroring the Gleam client.
No new auth scheme — clients carry whatever credential the server requires (bearer / mTLS per AW); they do not invent one.
